message.content:
The smell hit them halfway down the hall.

Not the vague, sour reek they’d both come to expect from death, but something sharper, heavier—like a butcher’s shop left to rot in a greenhouse. Hot copper and wet animal, riding the damp air of the old warehouse and growing stronger with each step.

Hale paused at the threshold, one hand on the doorframe. The crime-scene techs had thrown open the broken windows, but the air inside barely moved. It just sat there, thick as soup, pressing the stench into the back of his throat.

“Ready?” Morales asked quietly.

He nodded, and they went in.

The body was the first thing the eye found, because the room had been built to draw attention to it. The concrete floor was mostly bare—no crates, no machinery, no clutter—just an empty square of space and, at the center, a metal chair under a single, naked bulb. Everything else fell away in the glare.

The woman was tied upright, ankles lashed to the chair legs, wrists pulled back and bound so tight behind the slats that the skin around the cord was glossy and swollen. Her head slumped forward at an angle that suggested the neck had given out long after the rest of her had. Strands of hair, dark with sweat and something thicker, hung like seaweed across her face.

Her shirt was gone. In its place, the skin of her torso had been turned into something deliberate. Not a frantic slash or a crude rip, but a clean, purposeful opening from the base of her sternum to just below the navel, edges rolled slightly outward as if on display. It wasn’t the ragged chaos of an animal mauling; it was controlled. Chosen.

The bulb above caught the wet surfaces inside, turning them into shades of lacquered red and blacker maroon. Loops of bowel glistened where they’d been drawn partway out and arranged over her lap like someone laying out cables, a careful S-curve here, a deliberate twist there. Each coil shone with a thin skim of fluid that caught the light and gathered into slow, fat drops.

They fell steadily, one after another, into the pool growing beneath the chair.

Hale forced himself to take in the scene without flinching, cataloguing instead of reacting. The liver had been lifted almost reverently and placed on a metal tray at her feet, as though it were an object of value, not meat. The heart wasn’t in the chest cavity at all; it sat on her right thigh like an offering, a palm-sized knot of muscle still slick, the aorta trailing down into the ruin of her torso like a cut cable.

The blood hadn’t sprayed wildly, hadn’t painted the walls in arc. It had flowed. It had run and poured and soaked, darkening the front of the chair, tracing the grain of the metal welds, gathering in the tiny imperfections of the concrete before joining the main pool. The stain was almost symmetrical, fanning out from the chair in a rough halo. At its farthest edge, it thinned to a ragged line, tiny tributaries reaching for drains and cracks.

They’d put a shallow metal pan under the chair, but only after the fact. It was nearly full now, the surface trembling each time another drop fell from the dangling loops of intestine or the curved edge of a rib. Every quiet plink sounded too loud in the cavernous room.

Morales stepped closer, one hand pressed under her nose as if that might dull the smell. It didn’t. Up close, the odor eclipsed everything else—sourness layered over iron, something fatty and almost sweet, a hot, animal funk that turned the air into something you could feel on your skin. There was a faint chemical edge beneath it, antiseptic or cleaning alcohol, but it was drowning under the tide of decay.

“She was alive when he did this,” Morales said, voice thin. “See here—” She pointed with a gloved hand to the margins of the incision. “Petechiae. And there’s… clotting, you can see it forming along the edges. He took his time.”

Hale’s eyes drifted to the wall behind the chair. Someone—no, not someone, the same someone—had painted shapes there with the same blood that coated the floor. Not random streaks. Symbols. Broad, looping lines that might have been circles broken by straight, clean strokes. The brush had been a bare hand or a gloved palm dragged deliberately, leaving ridged swaths like finger marks in wet clay.

The patterns framed the body. Two wide arcs bracketed her shoulders, curving up and meeting just above her bowed head. From that point, lines radiated outward like rays, reaching the far corners of the concrete. The
